--- Page 1 ---
Masked Unfairness: Hiding Causality within Zero ATE
Zou Yang1, Sophia Xiao1, and Bijan Mazaheri1,2
1Thayer School of Engineering, Dartmouth College, Hanover, New Hampshire, USA
2Schmidt Center, Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard, Cambridge, Massachusetts, USA
Abstract
Recent work has proposed powerful frameworks,
rooted in causal theory, to quantify fairness. Causal
inference has primarily emphasized the detection of
average treatment effects (ATEs), and subsequent no-
tions of fairness have inherited this focus.
In this
paper, we build on previous concerns about regula-
tion based on averages. In particular, we formulate
the “causal masking problem” as a linear program
that optimizes an alternative objective, such as max-
imizing profit or minimizing crime, while retaining
a zero ATE (i.e., the ATE between a protected at-
tribute and a decision). By studying the capabilities
and limitations of causal masking, we show that op-
timization under ATE-based regulation may induce
significant unequal treatment. We demonstrate that
the divergence between true and causally masked
fairness is driven by confounding, underscoring the
importance of full conditional-independence testing
when assessing fairness. Finally, we discuss statisti-
cal and information-theoretic limitations that make
causally masked solutions very difficult to detect, al-
lowing them to persist for long periods. These results
argue that we must regulate fairness at the model-
level, rather than at the decision level.
1
Introduction
While modern methods for AI and machine learning
are fantastic optimizers, they exhibit concerning po-
tential for bias and unfair inference [Ferrara, 2024].
Angwin et al. [2022], for example, showed that many
statistical tools for parole are biased against minor-
ity groups. As such, it is essential to develop formal
notions of fairness that can be integrated into increas-
ingly automated decision processes.
Numerous works have attempted to formalize fair-
ness over the past decade [Dwork et al., 2012]. One
emerging thread of interest is the use of causal prin-
ciples to distinguish systematically biased decisions
from “confounded” imbalances driven by population
and demographic differences. For example, hiring pri-
marily male luggage handlers may still be considered
“fair” so long as the decisions are based on the phys-
ical strength of job applicants and not a direct bias
against women. However, explicitly denying employ-
ment to strong women is evidence of direct unfair
bias. The framework introduced in Nabi and Shpitser
[2018] states that sex should not have a causal effect
on manager hiring decisions.
A causal notion of fairness is advantageous be-
cause it enables the application of an increasingly
rich body of literature on causal inference. Potential
outcome [Imbens and Rubin, 2015] and graphically-
based [Pearl, 2009] models have revolutionized our
ability to determine the causal effects of an interven-
tion, usually expressed as the average effect across a
population.
The ladder of causality places an important dis-
tinction between population-level effects (e.g, “What
will happen, on average, if I take an action?”) and
counterfactuals (e.g., “What would have happened
if I had done something differently”) [Pearl, 2009].
Counterfactuals exist at the pinnacle of causality be-
cause they represent the individual treatment effect
(ITE) across multiple worlds, while the second rung
only requires recovery of the average treatment effect
(ATE) across a population.
Although ITEs are strictly more informative than
ATEs, stricter parametric assumptions on the equa-
tions governing the system are required for their
identification.
Meanwhile, ATEs can be computed
nonparametrically in many settings, only requiring
knowledge of a network of dependencies, called a
structural causal model (SCM), and a joint prob-
ability distribution.
As such, much of the causal
literature has focused on the second rung of the
causal ladder:
computing ATEs and conditional
ATEs (CATEs) within more restricted populations.
Building a formal notion of fairness on the liter-
ature of causal inference implicitly inherits its gaps
and insufficiencies. In the context of this paper, one
1
arXiv:2603.06984v1  [stat.ML]  7 Mar 2026


--- Page 2 ---
such insufficiency is the focus on ATEs. In fact, pre-
vious work has pointed out that average and even
parity-based notions of fairness can lead to unequal
treatment of subgroups through “subset targeting”
[Dwork et al., 2012].
To address this, Kim and
Zubizarreta [2023] proposed a regularization frame-
work to ensure that the regression results are ho-
mogeneously fair. Still, enforcing the utilization of
such techniques is difficult.
Regulators often focus
on the outcomes of potentially biased models rather
than their algorithmic output, making statistical de-
tectability critical.
All
current
causality-based
(and
many
non-
causality-based) methods for detecting unfair treat-
ment focus on average population-level fairness. This
paper studies the task of evading these fairness cri-
teria (intentionally or unintentionally) by optimizing
an auxiliary goal. Our findings demonstrate the im-
portance of integrating modern notions of heteroge-
neous treatment effects (HTEs) [Wager and Athey,
2018], mixtures of treatment effects (MTEs) [Maza-
heri et al., 2025], tests of conditional independence
[Zhang et al., 2011, de Morais Andrade et al., 2014,
Canonne et al., 2018, Runge, 2018] into the study
of fairness.
We caution that conditional indepen-
dence testing is an information-theoretically difficult
task, and many tests for heterogeneous dependence
impose strict assumptions and require large datasets
[Canonne et al., 2018, Shah and Peters, 2020, He
et al., 2025]. This may allow causally masked policies
to evade statistical detection if regulation is left at the
level of decision data. Instead, we suggest building
fairness regulations on the model outputs and enforc-
ing them through “in-process mechanisms” [Pessach
and Shmueli, 2022].
1.1
Summary of Contributions
In this paper, we introduce the “causal masking”
problem, which seeks to optimize an auxiliary re-
ward while satisfying a zero ATE between two vari-
ables of interest. A motivational example maintains a
zero ATE between a protected attribute and admis-
sions decisions, while optimizing for student gradu-
ations (or graduation rate). Similar structures may
emerge in other settings, such as minimizing recidi-
vism while maintaining a zero ATE for race on parole
decisions. We show that globally regulated optimiza-
tion can lead to significant violations of stratified fair-
ness, despite appearing fair to average-based fairness
metrics. This behavior is empirically demonstrated in
Section 7 for the parole setting using the well-known
COMPAS dataset from ProPublica [Angwin et al.,
2022].
Code for both real-data and synthetic data
experiments is provided in an anonymized GitHub
link.
Under some simplifying assumptions, the causal
masking problem can be formulated as a linear pro-
gram (LP), which we outline in Section 4. This pro-
vides a rigorous theoretical foundation for studying
the role of confounding variables in the disparity be-
tween “true” and “masked” fairness. We provide the-
orems in Section 5 that illustrate how causal mask-
ing is inherently driven by the statistical dependence
between a public source of information and the pro-
tected attribute, as well as by the dependence be-
tween that information and the optimized outcome.
Causal masking may be malicious or a naturally
occurring consequence of optimization, as shown by
genericity arguments in Section 5 with more details
in Appendix B. To mitigate the risk of causal mask-
ing, we advocate for fairness metrics based on full
conditional-independence testing. This approach is
inherently data-intensive, and systems that are heav-
ily confounded may be causally masked for extended
periods before we collect sufficient data to detect their
behavior. This underscores the need for proactive and
model-level regulatory action, rather than a (poten-
tially easier) reactive approach based on data from
decision outcomes.
1.2
Related Works
Formalizing Fairness.
Many works have formal-
ized definitions of fairness as independence proper-
ties between the protected attributes and the deci-
sion [Pessach and Shmueli, 2022]. Statistical parity
holds if the protected attribute is independent from
the decision [Dwork et al., 2012]. Conditional statis-
tical parity relaxed this notion to parity across sub-
groups [Corbett-Davies et al., 2017]. Nabi and Sh-
pitser [2018] formalizes the problem within causal ef-
fects, while Kusner et al. [2017] discusses a related
notion based on counterfactuals (at the ITE level),
which uses structural equations. Path-specific fair-
ness has refined these notions to decompose differ-
ent mechanisms of unfair treatment [Chiappa, 2019,
Pleˇcko and Bareinboim, 2024].
Our work studies the detection of fairness viola-
tions in decision-making. Kusner et al. [2017]’s use
of learned functions in a model is in line with our sug-
gestion to regulate models instead of decisions, where
we are not vulnerable to statistical weaknesses of con-
ditional independence testing.
Optimization and Fairness.
Dwork et al. [2012]
was the first to formalize fairness as a constraint
within optimization, defining an LP that optimizes
2


--- Page 3 ---
decisions over individuals.
Corbett-Davies et al.
[2017] studied this in more detail, quantifying the
“cost of fairness” in the objective function.
Also
within the scope of optimization, Corbett-Davies
et al. [2023] studied the geometry of fairness con-
straints and showed that fairness constraints often
yield suboptimal solutions, hurting the groups they
intended to help. Our optimization problem differs
only slightly, focusing on decision rates across groups
rather than decisions on individuals. We study the
effect of ATE-based constraints, which were not ad-
dressed in these earlier works.
Nabi et al. [2019]
presented an alternative formulation of policy opti-
mization for online reinforcement learning with an
algorithmic focus, using the path-specific effect con-
straints of Nabi and Shpitser [2018] (which are also
averages). Our paper is primarily concerned with the
choices of constraints and is therefore complementary.
Fairness in Predictions.
A parallel line of work
has studied fairness with respect to predictions,
e.g., equalized odds[Barocas et al., 2019], equal op-
portunity[Hardt et al., 2016], and predictive parity
[Chouldechova, 2017] and its relationship to calibra-
tion [Pleiss et al., 2017].
Principal fairness, based
on potential outcomes, states that individuals who
are affected similarly by a decision should be treated
similarly [Imai and Jiang, 2023].
Computing HTEs.
There are many methods for
computing heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs),
which may be useful for detecting causal masking.
Wager and Athey [2018] developed causal forests to
determine HTEs within clusters, which pairs closely
with a cluster-specific optimization that likely assigns
a different CATE to each cluster. Mixtures of treat-
ment effects (MTEs) have also been studied to learn
heterogeneity with significant overlap in a covariate
space [Mazaheri et al., 2025].
While our work fo-
cuses on naturally- and adversarially-occurring het-
erogeneity that is separated by observed covariates,
it is possible that subgroups that are indistinguish-
able by observed covariates may still receive unequal
treatment. MTE-based methods may extend our con-
cepts to counterfactual notions of fairness.
We focus solely on detecting unfairness rather than
computing its distribution, for which hypothesis test-
ing is a sufficient statistical tool.
Within this line
of work, there are a number of heterogeneity tests
that focus on computational and statistical challenges
associated with continuous variables and regression
[Dahabreh et al., 2017, Huitfeldt et al., 2019]. We ar-
gue that such tests will become critical for assessing
fairness in human decisions, and when the underlying
target frequencies given by a model are unknown.
2
Preliminaries
Notation.
We will use capital Roman letters to de-
note random variables and their corresponding low-
ercase letters to denote their realizations.
We use
the short-hand xi to indicate the realization of a ran-
dom variable X = i. Lists of random variables will
be bolded, e.g., X, and vectors of parameters will be
denoted with vector notation, e.g., ⃗α.
ATEs and CATEs.
The average treatment effect
of treatment P ∈{0, 1} on outcome D ∈{0, 1} is de-
fined with respect to an adjustment set X such that
cases with identical X = x values are considered “ex-
changeable” [Pearl, 2009]. The backdoor adjustment
formula is
ATE := E[D(1) −D(0)]
=
X
x
Pr(x)[E[D | x, p1] −E[D | x, p0]].
(1)
where D(p) is the “potential outcome” represent-
ing the random variable from which D is drawn when
P = p [Rubin, 2005]. E[D(1)−D(0)] is also sometimes
written as the difference between two “do interven-
tions,” E[D | do(P = 1)] −E[D | do(P = 1)] [Pearl,
2009].
The terms of the sum in Eq. (1) can be considered
“conditional” average treatment effects (CATEs) be-
cause they represent the average change in the ex-
pectation of D within a X = x subgroup. A crit-
ical concept in the estimation of ATEs and CATEs
is the propensity score.
In the context of this pa-
per, the propensity score represents the likelihood of
an individual belonging to a specific protected group
P, conditional on their covariates X, i.e., Pr(p | x).
Propensity scores will appear in our analysis of im-
provements from causal masking in Section 5.
Causal Graphs.
Causal masking settings will be
described using “structural causal models” with di-
rected edges representing causal relationships be-
tween the random variables (the vertices).
A crit-
ical insight from the work of Pearl [2009] is that
the causal structure informs the statistical indepen-
dencies of the random variables via graphical d-
separation rules.
The faithfulness assumption, of-
ten utilized when learning causal structures [Vowels
et al., 2022], states that “d-connected” or causally
linked variables are statistically dependent. Faithful-
ness holds with full Lebesgue measure [Meek, 1995],
3


--- Page 4 ---
but Uhler et al. [2013] showed that the set of dis-
tributions that are ε “close” to faithfulness viola-
tions grows exponentially with ε, meaning that finite-
sample regimes that test for conditional independence
with significance thresholds are much more likely to
struggle with the consequences of “near” faithfulness
violations. This insight will become important when
discussing relaxations of the fairness and masking
constraints (defined later).
3
Motivating Example
We start by demonstrating how an optimization task
can be improved using a heterogenously unfair strat-
egy that maintains a zero ATE. While this section
uses a toy distribution, Section 7 shows similar phe-
nomena on the real-world COMPAS dataset.
Setup Distribution.
Suppose a university seeks a
model that informs admissions decisions (D = 1 cor-
responds to admission) for two different departments,
X ∈{0, 1}, that is “fair” for some protected attribute
P ∈{0, 1}. We are motivated by the famous Berke-
ley admissions case [Bickel et al., 1975], and give a
deliberately simple example in Table 1.
x
p
Pr(x, p)
Pr(Y = 1 | x, p)
Pr(Dmask = 1 | x, p)
0
0
1/15
.5
.5
0
1
9/15
.5
0
1
0
4/15
.25
0
1
1
1/15
1
1
Table 1: A description of a joint probability distribu-
tion on X, P, a list of graduation rates, and a masking
policy.
The goal is to maximize graduations (Y ∈{0, 1})
among admitted students. While the protected at-
tribute P should not be used, it correlates with
some public information like department X.
Since
department-level differences in admissions may skew
the distribution of the protected attribute in the stu-
dent body, the decisions may still be considered fair
so long as parity is achieved within each department.
Fair
Masked/Exploited
P
X
D
Y
P
X
D
Y
Figure 1: Two causal diagrams depicting the depen-
dencies between the random variables in our setting.
The undirected edge X −P captures optionality in
X ←P, or X →P.
Figure 1 gives causal diagrams for this setting, in-
cluding the absence of a P →D relationship for a
“truly fair” decision policy, giving conditional parity
within the context of P causing D, i.e., P ⊥⊥D | X.
We will describe two unfair policies: “Masking” and
“Exploiting”.
ATE as a Measure of Fairness.
A regulating
body wants to balance decisions to equal rates of
acceptance across minority and majority groups. A
“truly fair” set of decision rates, under this restric-
tion, would involve homogenously trivial group-level
CATEs for the effect of P on D. If the covariate X
gives us exchangeability1, this corresponds to
E(Y | x0, p1) −E(Y | x0, p0) = 0,
E(Y | x1, p1) −E(Y | x1, p0) = 0.
“Global” or “average” fairness is a much milder re-
striction:
ATE = Pr(x0)(E(Y | x0, p1) −E(Y | x0, p0))
+ Pr(x1)(E(Y | x1, p1) −E(Y | x1, p0)) = 0.
Evading ATE Detection.
Notice that all fair ad-
mission policies involve a graduation rate of no more
than 50%. However, an optimizer that uses minor-
ity status (P) can do better by granting admission
only to non-minority (P = 1) applicants in the sec-
ond department (X = 1), achieving a perfect gradu-
ation rate. Such a “exploitative” strategy utilizes P
and clearly fails ATE-based fairness metrics, with an
ATE of 1/3.
Now consider the “masking” strategy outlined as
Dmask in Table 1. Notice that it achieves an ATE
of 0 by strategically offsetting P = 0, X = 1 cases
with P = 1, X = 0 cases.
This is unfair to both
the non-minority and minority groups in the X =
1, P = 0 and X = 0, P = 1 cases.
Still, such a
strategy evades the detection of a na¨ıve ATE-based
regulator. Furthermore, such an approach achieves
a better graduation rate (5/6) than fairness (1/2). As
such, an optimized strategy, whether by humans or
by machines, is likely to incur some or all of this bias.
Other Formulations.
This setup is general and
may emerge in many settings. For example, courts
may seek to make fair parole decisions (Y ) unbiased
by a protected status (P) when controlling for crime
details (X). We perform such a study on real data in
Section 7.
1This is the case for the graph specified in Figure 3, as X
is a “backdoor adjustment set.” See Pearl [2009] for a detailed
discussion.
4


--- Page 5 ---
4
Causal Masking
Motivated by the previous section, we formalize the
causal masking problem as a linear program (LP).
Suppose that we have a decision D ∈{0, 1} represent-
ing “when to participate.” Examples of participation
include granting parole, making a hire, or admitting
a student. The goal of these decisions is to maximize
a reward Y when D = 1, i.e., E[Y |d1]. An equivalent
formulation is to maximize E[Y |d1] Pr(d1) subject to
the participation rate, Pr(d1), being constant.
The features on which we may base our policy are
divided into P, which is protected, and X, which is
unprotected. For simplicity, we treat X ∈{1, . . . , k}
and P ∈{0, 1} as scalars. Continuous and multivari-
ate X can be reduced to this setting using clustering
algorithms, such as k-means. While this reduces the
information capacity of the more complicated X, the
results obtained in the coarsened X serve as a lower
bound on the achievable objective in the more general
case, with the parameter k providing a natural way
to study the problem’s “resolution”. It is also pos-
sible to extend to multivariate and continuous P us-
ing multivariate and continuous interventions [Pearl,
2009], but such extensions are beyond the scope of
this work. Our choice of binary Y is arbitrary, since
we only maximize its expectation and conditional ex-
pectation.
A decision policy is given by a function α(x, p)
that maps the support of X, P onto [0, 1], indicat-
ing the “rates of participation” for each x, u pair.
Such a decision policy yields a random variable with
probabilities given by these participation rates, D ∼
Bernoulli(α(x, p)). Under this specification, we may
now assume without loss of generality that we are
optimizing a deterministic function of x, p, which we
call γ(x, p) = E[Y |x, p]. Our goal is to select an “op-
timal” decision policy that maximizes E[Y |d1] Pr(d1)
subject to a constraint of constant participation rate
Pr(d1).
4.1
LP Formulation
When X, P are discrete, a policy is represented by
a vector of frequencies αx,p = Pr(d1 | x, p), and a
reward function is represented by a vector of expected
rewards γx,p = E[Y |x, p], each of which is indexed by
the values of X and P. This also allows us to index
Pr(x, p) = πx,p.
Using the law of total expectation and D
⊥⊥
Y | X, P, the objective is:
W(D) = E[E[Y · D | X, P]] =
X
x,p
γx,pαx,pπx,p.
The following three optimization problems formalize
“exploitation”, “fairness”, and “masking” as linear
programs. In order maintain equivalence with maxi-
mizing the rate of wins, we add a participation con-
straint P
x,p αx,pπx,p = ρ.
Proposition 4.1 (Optimal Exploit Policy). The
“exploit” approach optimally uses X, P without fur-
ther constraints:
max
{αx,p}
W(D) =
k
X
x=1
γxi,1αx,1πx,1 + γx,0αx,0πx,0
s.t.
0 ⩽αx,p ⩽1
∀x, p
X
x,p
αx,pπx,p = ρ.
The optimal policy participates greedily with nonzero
αx,p in (x∗
1, p∗
1) with the highest possible reward func-
tion, (x∗
1, p∗
1) = arg maxx,p γ(x, p), then the second
highest (x∗
2, p∗
2), and so on until the participation rate
is exhausted.
Proposition 4.2 (Optimal ε-Fair Policy). The op-
timal εfair-fair policy, Dfair(εfair), imposes a “within
εfair” fairness constraint of P ⊥⊥D | X, requiring
decision parity across all strata, X = x. The addi-
tional constraint is
s.t.
−εfair ⩽αx,1 −αx,0 ⩽εfair
∀x = 1, . . . , k.
The optimal solution for ε = 0 can be expressed using
wavg
x
:= E[Y | x] = γx,1 Pr(p1|x) + γx,0 Pr(p0|x).
Let x∗

, and all following x∗

so that x∗

k rep-
resent an ordering of the best to worst settings. The
optimal fair policy is to always participate in context
x∗
1, then x∗
2, and so on until we have exhausted the
participation rate.
Proposition 4.3 (Optimal ε-Masking Policy). The
optimal εmask-masking policy, Dmask(εmask), is the
solution to the LP with a masking constraint that
bounds the ATE:
max
{αx,p}
W(D) =
k
X
x=1
γx,1αx,1πx,1 + γx,0αx,0πx,0
s.t.
−εmask ⩽
X
x
(πx,0 + πx,1)(αx,1 −αx,0) ⩽εmask
0 ⩽αx,p ⩽1
∀x, p
X
x,p
αx,pπx,p = ρ.
5


--- Page 6 ---
Both ε-fairness and ε-masking will usually be con-
sidered in ε = 0 settings, leading to perfect fairness
and perfect masking strategies Dfair and Dmask re-
spectively. Relaxations with nonzero ε will be used
to discuss finite-sample behavior in Section 6. Notice
that the masking constraint is satisfied by the fair-
ness constraint. As such, optimal masking is always
at least as good as optimal fairness.
5
Theoretical Results
This section establishes key properties of causal
masking. We lower-bound the benefit of causal mask-
ing relative to the fair solution and ultimately argue
that masking is a natural outcome of optimization
when subject to a zero ATE constraint, even when
the decision-maker is not acting adversarially.
All
proofs are in Appendix A.
5.1
Improvements from Masking
The optimal fair policy, Dfair, is a “water-filling” al-
gorithm that selects the best strata by average re-
ward until the participation budget ρ is met. If ρ is
sufficiently small, then this procedure simply chooses
the single best stratum x∗= arg maxx wavg
x
. Starting
from this baseline, a decision-maker could be tempted
to make an “unfair” swap to increase utility. For ex-
ample, they could stop participating with group p = 0
in fair stratum xi and compensate by increasing par-
ticipation with group p = 0 in some other stratum
xj. Such a change must then scale the participation
rate to keep it constant.
This swap-and-scale pro-
cedure provides a lower bound on the performance
gap that is valid in “low participation rate” (small ρ)
scenarios.
Definition 5.1. Define the ”arbitrage rate of return”
for a policy that trades participation between states
(i, p) and (j, p) as:
Rp(j) :=
wi,p + wj,p
Pr(p|xi) + Pr(p|xj)
where p = |1 −p| and wx,p = γx,p Pr(p|x).
Theorem 5.2 (The Gap between Masking and Fair-
ness). Assume the participation rate ρ is sufficiently
small such that the optimal fair policy, Dfair, only
participates in the best stratum x∗= xi. The perfor-
mance gap, ∆= W(Dmask) −W(Dfair), is bounded
by:
∆⩾ρ · max

0,
max
j̸=i,p∈{0,1} {Rp(j) −wavg
i
}

To see this theorem in action, we apply it to the
motivating example in Table 1. First, we calculate
that the best stratum for the fair policy is x∗= x0,
with an average reward of wavg
0
= 0.5. The most prof-
itable arbitrage opportunity involves trading between
state (i, p) = (0, 0) and (j, p) = (1, 1). This yields
an arbitrage rate of return R1(1) =
5
6.
Plugging
these values into Theorem 5.2, the performance gap
is bounded by ∆⩾ρ·(5/6−0.5) = ρ/3. The specific
masking policy shown in the table has a participation
rate of ρ = 0.1. For this value, the theorem guaran-
tees a minimum gap of 0.1/3 = 1/30. Remarkably, for
this example, the bound is tight. The optimal fair
policy yields a utility of W(Dfair) = 0.1 × 0.5 = 1/20,
while the Dmask policy yields a utility of W(Dmask) =
1/12. The true gap is 1/12−1/20 = 1/30, exactly match-
ing our lower bound.
5.2
The Role of the Observed X
The performance gap between the masking and fair
policies is a direct consequence of data dependencies.
A gap can only emerge if the optimizer can exploit ei-
ther (1) demographic disparities between strata or (2)
heterogeneous outcome patterns across those strata.
These correspond to the dependencies P ̸⊥⊥X (con-
founding) and X ̸⊥⊥Y | P (heterogeneity), respec-
tively.
From Theorem 5.2, a gap can exist if the arbitrage
rate of return, Rp(j), exceeds the fair policy’s return,
wavg
i
; the existence of such a gap is characterized by
two sufficient conditions:
1. Heterogeneity (X ̸⊥⊥Y | P): Even if there is
no confounding (P ⊥⊥X, so Pr(P = p|x) = Pr(P =
p)), the rewards γx,p can still vary across strata x.
The context-weighted rewards wx,p = γx,p Pr(P = p)
will therefore also vary.
The arbitrage rate Rp(j)
becomes a mix of different reward terms (e.g., from
γi,1−p and γj,p) that can easily outperform the sim-
ple average reward wavg
i
of the fair policy’s chosen
stratum. Therefore, heterogeneity in outcomes is a
sufficient driver for a performance gap.
2. Confounding (P ̸⊥⊥X): Even if there are no
heterogeneous effects (X ⊥⊥Y | P, so γx,p = γp), the
propensity scores Pr(p|x) can vary across strata x. In
this case, the arbitrage rate of return is
Rp(j) = γ1−p Pr(p|xi) + γp Pr(p|xj)
Pr(p|xi) + Pr(p|xj)
.
This is a weighted average of the base rewards γp and
γ1−p. The fair policy’s return, wavg
i
= γ0 Pr(p0|xi) +
γ1 Pr(p1|xi), is also a weighted average. Because the
weights (the propensities) in Rp(j) are drawn from
the different propensities of strata i and j, these two
6


--- Page 7 ---
averages will not be equal in general. The masking
policy can exploit this difference, proving that con-
founding is also a sufficient driver for a performance
gap.
The following theorems formalize that a gap exists
if and only if either of these two dependencies holds.
Theorem 5.3 (Necessary Conditions for a Perfor-
mance Gap). Let ∆= W(Dmask) −W(Dfair) be the
performance gap. If P ⊥⊥X AND X ⊥⊥Y | P, then
the performance gap is identically zero (∆= 0).
Theorem 5.4 (Sufficient Conditions for a Generic
Gap). If either confounding exists (P ̸⊥⊥X) or het-
erogeneous effects exist (X ̸⊥⊥Y | P), then for a
set of world parameters (π, γ) of full Lebesgue mea-
sure satisfying this condition, the performance gap is
strictly positive (∆> 0).
The results of Theorem 5.3 and Theorem 5.4 can be
summarized as a relaxation of the graph initially in-
troduced in Figure 1, now shown in Figure 2. The
causal graphs on the left represent settings where
causal masking is generically beneficial due to the
presence of either an X →Y path or an X −P con-
founding path.
The graph on the right cannot be
improved because both dependencies are absent, so
the masked solution gives the same utility as the fair
solution.
W(Dmask) > W(Dfair) (generically)
W(Dmask) = W(Dfair)
P
X
D
Y
P
X
D
Y
P
X
D
Y
Figure 2: Two relaxations that allow improvements
from causal masking (on the left), and one where
there is no improvement from fairness (on the right).
5.3
The Role of Regulation
Testing for non-zero ATEs can be done with a simple
z-test whose sample complexity does not depend on k,
so the statistical power does not get worse when k in-
creases. Causally masked solutions require a full het-
erogeneity test, which partitions the data into X = x
strata and thus reduces the statistical power for larger
k. Both tests are given in Appendix D. This implies
that unfair solutions that are not causally masked are
likely to be easier to detect than those that are. This
subsection argues that ATE-based regulation does
not necessarily improve fairness and instead pushes
solutions into this “hard-to-detect” realm.
In the infinite sample regime, we give basic gener-
icity results in Appendix B, summarized in three
points:
1. Without regulation, solutions are unfair and un-
masked.
2. Optimized solutions are also unfair and un-
masked.
3. ATE-based constraints still result in unfair solu-
tions.
This shows that imposing zero ATE regulations al-
most inevitably leads to causally masked policies, for
which fairness is more difficult to detect than in un-
regulated settings.
We can also quantify the size of the feasible sets. In
Appendix C, we show that feasible fair solutions are
a strict subset of feasible masking solutions, and that
ε-fair solutions have a significantly smaller volume
when ε is relaxed to handle finite-sample regimes.
This volume also grows significantly slower (O(εk)
vs. O(ε)). As such, we must relax fairness to a far
more detectable εfair to meaningfully improve our ob-
jective, whereas small (and likely undetectable) relax-
ations of masking provide significant flexibility. The
next section demonstrates that optimization does, in-
deed, take advantage of this growth in the feasible
sets.
6
Synthetic Experiments
This section quantitatively reinforces the intuition
that relaxing fairness constraints yields significant
utility, even under zero-ATE regulations. We demon-
strate that a very small and likely undetectable relax-
ation of a zero-ATE constraint is capable of signifi-
cant utility gain.
To do this, we performed a synthetic data exper-
iment on 100,000 uniformly sampled models with ⃗π
in the simplex, ⃗γ ∈[0, 1]2k, ρ = .25 and Dexploit,
Dfair(εfair), and Dmask(εmask) determined by the so-
lutions to their respective LPs. The results are re-
ported in Figure 3.
The achieved objective (i.e., “performance”) of
each LP is normalized so that 0 corresponds to
W(Dfair(0)) and 1 corresponds to W(Dexploit).
To
illustrate the role of k, we repeat the experiment for
k = 2 (top row) and k = 10 (bottom row).
The first column shows a moderate improvement in
optimization when the fairness constraint is relaxed
εfair. This improvement is dwarfed in the second col-
umn, where a relaxation of εmask increases the perfor-
mance much faster than a relaxation of the fairness
constraint. This emphasizes that large gains can be
obtained with very little change in a metric that is
7


--- Page 8 ---
Figure 3: Synthetic data experiments showing that relaxing masking and fairness yield tempting performance
improvements.
already statistically difficult to detect. This effect is
magnified for larger k.
We also studied a fourth optimization problem,
which includes both a masking constraint with
εmask = 0 and a fairness constraint with variable
εfair, whose results are shown in the third column.
Within these completely masked policies, we receive
an approximately linear benefit from relaxing the fair-
ness constraint. This demonstrates the temptation to
move from fair to unfair policies when only regulated
by an ATE-based masking constraint.
7
Real Data Demonstration
In this section, we simulate a parole policy based
on causal masking while optimizing for minimal 2-
year recidivism. We show that the resulting outcome
evades detection of its ATE while remaining unfair.
Due to the statistical difficulties in conditional inde-
pendence testing, the new masked policy persists for
a long time until there is sufficient data to detect its
(stratified) unfairness.
7.1
Setup
We simulate a parole policy D, where D = 1 indicates
a release and D = 0 indicates a denied parole. The
goal is to minimize the expected number of recidi-
visms, E[Y | d1] Pr(d1), where Y is 2-year recidivism,
while remaining fair with respect to minority status
(P). We impose a minimum release-rate constraint
Pr(d1) = P
x,p πx,pα(x, p) ⩾ρ, where πx,p = Pr(x, p)
is estimated from the data and ρ = 0.1.
For X, we select the following variables: the age
category of defendants, the prior criminal records of
defendants, the charge degree of defendants, and the
sex of defendants.
To determine the stratification
X, such that variables are discretized using quantile-
based binning at various resolutions.
We evaluate each policy by testing two nested null
hypotheses that correspond to the masking and fair
conditions.
The first, a test of global fairness, as-
sesses whether the Average Treatment Effect (ATE)
of the protected attribute P on the decision D is zero
across the population. The second, a stricter test of
stratified fairness, assesses whether this is true within
every single stratum X = x. Formally, using the pol-
icy rates αx,p, we define these as:
Global Fairness : HATE : Pk
x=1 Pr(X = x)(αx,1 −
αx,0) = 0. This ATE-based regulatory check ensures
a policy appears fair “on average.”
Stratified Fairness: HCATE : αx,1 −αx,0 = 0, ∀x.
The stronger, true fairness condition; if HCATE holds,
HATE is satisfied automatically.
Rejecting local fairness while accepting global fair-
ness is the definitive signature of a successfully
masked policy. To test these hypotheses, we utilize a
8


--- Page 9 ---
5
10
15
20
25
30
k (number of strata)
102
103
104
First rejection n (log scale)
First n to Reject HATE
5
10
15
20
25
30
k (number of strata)
102
103
104
First rejection n (log scale)
First n to Reject HCATE
5
10
15
20
25
30
k (number of strata)
102
103
104
First rejection n (log scale)
First n to Reject Either Hypothesis
5
10
15
20
25
30
k (number of strata)
0
20
40
60
80
100
Cumulative unfairness
Total Unfairness Until
Either Test Rejects
Detection Difficulty vs Number of Strata k
EXPLOIT
FAIR
MASK
Nmax = 10,000
Figure 4: Simulations showing the average (+- standard error) sample size needed to reject each null hy-
pothesis for all three strategies.
z-test for HATE and χ2 test for HCATE, both of which
are outlined in Appendix D.
7.2
Results
In Figure 4, we performed our statistical tests on
policies applied to successively larger datasets until
we rejected HATE, HCATE, or either, simulating the
“longevity” of each strategy.
In all cases, the fair
strategy is rejected last (as happens by chance), and
the exploit strategy is rejected first. Notice that the
masking strategy rejects HATE at similar times to the
fair strategy, while the exploit strategy is quickly dis-
covered to be unfair.
In the middle two plots, we
see that, even with all forms of testing, the masking
strategy is allowed to persist over five times longer
than the exploit strategy, with this gap worsening
for larger k. Finally, on the right, we see that the
longevity of the masked solution coupled with its
hidden unfairness contributes the most “total unfair-
ness” as measured by adding αx,1−αx,0 (the disparity
of decision probabilities) for each candidate until the
policy is “caught” as unfair.
8
Discussion
This paper introduces the causal masking problem,
formulates it as an LP, and examines its properties.
We showed that ATE-based regulation almost always
leads optimizers to unfair solutions. Although this
unfairness is essentially certain, detecting such un-
fairness is information-theoretically difficult.
As a
result, these unfair models can remain undetected
for years. More complex observed covariates (larger
k) significantly widen the detectability gap between
generically unfair and masked unfair policies, while
also allowing more tempting masking solutions.
If
we restrict ourselves to decision statistics, we should
accept causal masking as inevitable and essentially
impossible to detect or regulate.
These limitations are fundamentally grounded in
the detectability of conditional independence and
therefore extend beyond causal notions of fairness to
any form of conditional parity. This has widespread
implications for many fairness applications, including
college admissions and criminal justice, as discussed
in this paper, as well as affirmative action [Holzer and
Neumark, 2000], and the gender wage gap [Pendakur
and Woodcock, 2010] — all of which have been pre-
dominantly analyzed using conditional (adjusted) av-
erages. Given these challenges, we argue that fairness
regulation should operate at the model level, where
the internal decision mechanism is accessible, rather
than solely at the level of observed decisions.
There are several natural extensions to our work.
The first involves the study of success rate maximiza-
tion with an unfixed participation rate. Examples in-
clude maximizing the win rate when counting cards
(while avoiding detection) or maximizing graduation
rate or average GPA. Such a problem is a linear-
fractional program with different properties.
Another important extension will involve general-
izing to measures of global dependence beyond ATEs,
such as global odds ratios, which can be detected us-
ing a CMH test [Zhang and Boos, 1997], or condi-
tional mutual information. These properties impose
distinct constraints, thereby opening avenues for sig-
nificant future work.
Acknowledgements
Support for Bijan Mazaheri and Sophia Xiao was pro-
vided by the Advanced Research Concepts (ARC)
COMPASS program, sponsored by the Defense Ad-
vanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA) under
agreement number HR001-25-3-0212.
Support for
Zou Yang was provided by a first-year fellowship by
the Thayer School of Engineering.

--- Page 13 ---
A
Deferred Proofs
A.1
Proof of Theorem 5.2
Proof. The proof proceeds by comparing the utility of the optimal fair policy to a specially constructed,
valid masking policy candidate, D′′
p,j.
1. Utility of the Optimal Fair Policy: Under the small ρ assumption, the optimal fair policy is to
participate only in stratum i with rates αi,1 = αi,0 = ρ/ Pr(X = i). The utility is:
W(Dfair) = ρ · wavg
i
.
2. Construct a Valid Masking Candidate Policy, D′′
p,j: We build a candidate policy from scratch
that is inspired by an arbitrage between states (i, 1) and (j, 0) (the argument for (i, 0) and (j, 1) is symmetric).
Step A: Define the Unscaled Arbitrage Policy (D′). Let’s construct an intermediate policy D′ that has
non-zero participation only for rates α′
i,1 and α′
j,0. To satisfy the zero ATE constraint, these rates must obey
the balance equation: Pr(X = i)α′
i,1 = Pr(X = j)α′
j,0. To make this policy as extreme as possible while
keeping all rates ⩽1, we set one rate to 1 and solve for the other. This results in the policy:
α′
i,1 = min(Pr(X = i), Pr(X = j))
Pr(X = i)
and
α′
j,0 = min(Pr(X = i), Pr(X = j))
Pr(X = j)
.
For example, if Pr(X = i) is larger, then α′
i,1 < 1 and α′
j,0 = 1.
Step B: Calculate the Rate of Return of D′. The utility of this unscaled policy is:
W(D′) = α′
i,1γi,1πi,1 + α′
j,0γj,0πj,0
= min(. . . )
Pr(xi) γi,1 Pr(P = 1|xi) Pr(xi) + min(. . . )
Pr(xj) γj,0 Pr(P = 0|xj) Pr(xj)
= min(Pr(X = i), Pr(X = j)) · [wi,1 + wj,0].
The participation rate of this policy is:
ρ′ = α′
i,1πi,1 + α′
j,0πj,0
= min(. . . )
Pr(X = i) Pr(P = 1|xi) Pr(xi) + min(. . . )
Pr(X = j) Pr(P = 0|xj) Pr(xj)
= min(Pr(X = i), Pr(xj)) · [Pr(P = 1|i) + Pr(P = 0|j)].
The key insight is that the rate of return, W(D′)/ρ′, is symmetric and simplifies because the “min” term
cancels out:
W(D′)
ρ′
=
wi,1 + wj,0
Pr(P = 1|i) + Pr(P = 0|j) = R0(j).
Step C: Scale to Create Valid Candidate D′′. We now define our final candidate policy D′′
0,j by scaling all
rates of D′ by a factor of (ρ/ρ′). This policy is guaranteed to be valid if we assume ρ is sufficiently small
(specifically, ρ ⩽ρ′). By construction, its participation rate is exactly ρ, and its utility is:
W(D′′
0,j) = ρ · R0(j).
3. Bounding the Gap: The optimal masking policy’s utility, W(Dmask), must be at least as high as the
utility of the best of these constructed candidates. The performance gap ∆is therefore bounded by:
∆= W(Dmask) −W(Dfair)
⩾
max
j̸=i,p∈{0,1}

W(D′′
p,j)
	
−W(Dfair)
= ρ ·
max
j̸=i,p∈{0,1} {Rp(j)} −ρ · wavg
i
.
Factoring out ρ and ensuring the gap is non-negative completes the proof.
13


--- Page 14 ---
A.2
Proof of Theorem 5.3
Proof. The two assumptions translate directly to our model parameters:
1. P ⊥⊥X =⇒Pr(P = p|X = x) = Pr(P = p) for all strata x.
2. X ⊥⊥Y | P =⇒E[Y |X = x, P = p] = E[Y |P = p]. In our notation, this means γx,p does not depend
on x, so we write it as γp.
Under these assumptions, the context-weighted reward is wx,p = γp Pr(P = p), which is constant for all
strata x. Consequently, the average reward wavg
x
= wx,0 + wx,1 = γ0 Pr(P = 0) + γ1 Pr(P = 1) is also
constant for all x. The fair policy is indifferent to which stratum it chooses. Let’s pick i.
Now, let’s analyze the arbitrage rate of return from Theorem 5.2:
Rp(j) =
wi,p + wj,p
Pr(P = p|xi) + Pr(P = p|xj)
= γp Pr(P = p) + γp Pr(P = p)
Pr(P = p) + Pr(P = p)
= γp Pr(P = p) + γp Pr(P = p) = wavg
i
.
Since Rp(j) = wavg
i
for all possible swaps, the lower bound on the gap from Theorem 5.2 is ρ · max(0, 0) = 0.
To show the gap is exactly zero, we note that any valid masking policy must satisfy the zero ATE constraint.
Under our assumptions, the decisions αx,p for a given p will be the same across all x (since wx,p is constant).
Let this policy be (α0, α1). The ATE constraint becomes (α1 −α0) P
x Pr(X = x) = α1 −α0 = 0. This
forces α1 = α0, meaning any optimal masking policy must also be a fair policy. Thus, W(Dmask) = W(Dfair)
and ∆= 0.
A.3
Proof of Theorem 5.4
Proof. The proof relies on showing that if either dependency holds, the ”no-gap” condition (∆= 0) imposes
a non-trivial equality constraint on the problem parameters, which defines a set of Lebesgue measure zero.
We use the bound from Theorem 5.2, which guarantees a positive gap if Rp(j) > wavg
i
for some swap (j, p),
where i = arg maxx wavg
x
.
Case 1: Confounding exists (P ̸⊥⊥X). Assume for simplicity X ⊥⊥Y | P (so γx,p = γp). The condition
P ̸⊥⊥X means that for some j ̸= i, Pr(P|j) ̸= Pr(P|i). The arbitrage return Rp(j) is a weighted average of
γp and γp, while wavg
i
is a different weighted average of the same values. These two averages will be equal
only if γp = γp or if the propensities align in a specific way. For generic, continuous parameters (π, γ), the
strict inequality Rp(j) ̸= wavg
i
will hold. There will almost surely be a swap for which the inequality is
favorable (Rp(j) > wavg
i
), creating a positive gap. The condition Rp(j) = wavg
i
defines a measure-zero set.
Case 2: Heterogeneous effects exist (X ̸⊥⊥Y | P). Assume for simplicity P ⊥⊥X (so Pr(P|x) =
Pr(P)). The condition X ̸⊥⊥Y | P means that for some p, γx,p is not constant across strata. The arbitrage
return Rp(j) involves terms like γj,p and γi,p, while the fair return wavg
i
involves γi,p and γi,p. For a generic
choice of reward parameters, it would be a measure-zero coincidence for the specific combination of terms
in Rp(j) to exactly equal the combination in wavg
i
. Therefore, a profitable swap will almost surely exist,
guaranteeing ∆> 0.
Since a strictly positive gap is generated (generically) if either condition holds, a zero-gap outcome repre-
sents a ”perfect alignment” of parameters that is broken by any small, random perturbation.
B
Genericity of Constraints and Solutions
B.1
Genericity of Constraints
A policy is a vector ⃗α = (αx1,0, αx1,1, . . . , αxk,0, αxk,1) in the policy space P = [0, 1]2k. We first show that
an arbitrarily chosen policy is almost never fair or masked.
Theorem B.1. The set of policies ⃗α ∈[0, 1]2k that satisfy (a) the fairness constraint or (b) the masking
constraint are sets of Lebesgue measure zero in P.
14


--- Page 15 ---
Proof. The space of all possible policies P = [0, 1]2k is a 2k-dimensional hypercube with volume 1.
(a) Fairness Constraint: The fairness constraint is A ⊥⊥U | X, which corresponds to the set of k linear
equality constraints:
αx,1 −αx,0 = 0
for each x ∈{x1, . . . , xk}.
These k constraints are independent, as each involves a disjoint pair of coordinates of ⃗α. Geometrically, the
set of policies satisfying these constraints is the intersection of k distinct hyperplanes with the hypercube
P. This intersection forms a k-dimensional manifold within the 2k-dimensional space P. For any k ⩾1, we
have k < 2k. A k-dimensional manifold has zero Lebesgue measure in a 2k-dimensional space. Thus, the set
of fair policies has measure zero.
(b) Masking Constraint: The masking constraint corresponds to a single linear equality:
k
X
x=1
Pr(X = x)(αx,1 −αx,0) = 0,
where Pr(X = x) = πx,0 + πx,1. Assuming not all Pr(X = x) are zero, this is a single non-trivial linear
constraint on the coordinates of ⃗α. Geometrically, this equation defines a (2k −1)-dimensional hyperplane
in R2k. The set of policies satisfying the constraint is the intersection of this hyperplane with the hypercube
P, which is a (2k −1)-dimensional manifold. Since 2k −1 < 2k, this set has Lebesgue measure zero in P.
B.2
Genericity of Optimal Solutions
We now consider the ”world parameters” (π, γ) which define the problem instance. Let the space of all such
parameters be Θ, a subset of a high-dimensional Euclidean space with a well-defined Lebesgue measure.
Theorem B.2. The set of world parameters (π, γ) ∈Θ for which the optimal exploit policy also satisfies
(a) the fairness constraint or (b) the masking constraint is a set of Lebesgue measure zero.
Proof. Let w′
x,u := γx,uπx,u. The objective is W(D) = P
x,u w′
x,uαx,u. For a generic choice of parameters,
the values of w′
x,u will be unique. In this case, the optimal exploit policy is a pure strategy: set αx∗,u∗= 1
for (x∗, u∗) = arg maxx,u w′
x,u, and all other αx,u = 0.
(a) Fairness Constraint: The pure exploit policy sets one coordinate to 1 and the rest to 0. For this
to satisfy the fairness constraints αx,1 = αx,0 for all x, every coordinate must be 0 (if all w′
x,u ⩽0) or 1. A
non-trivial pure exploit policy necessarily violates the constraint at stratum x∗, where it sets (αx∗,1, αx∗,0)
to either (1, 0) or (0, 1).
A non-pure exploit policy is optimal only if there is a tie for the maximum value of w′
x,u. For an optimal
exploit solution to be fair, it must be that a fair policy is also an optimal exploit solution. This requires a
specific alignment of parameters. For instance, if the optimal fair policy is αx′,1 = αx′,0 = 1 for some x′, then
for this to also be an optimal exploit solution, we need w′
x′,1 + w′
x′,0 ⩾w′
x,u for all (x, u) and for any other
fair policy. A necessary condition for such a policy to be expressible as an optimal exploit solution is a tie,
such as w′
x′,1 = w′
x′,0 being the two highest values. This equality, γx′,1πx′,1 = γx′,0πx′,0, is a single non-trivial
equality constraint on the continuous parameters (π, γ) and thus defines a set of Lebesgue measure zero in
Θ.
(b) Masking Constraint: Plugging the pure exploit policy αx∗,u∗= 1 into the masking constraint gives
Pr(X = x∗)(δu∗,1 −δu∗,0) = 0, which implies Pr(X = x∗) = 0, a trivial case. Thus, a pure exploit policy
cannot satisfy the masking constraint.
The constraint can only be satisfied if the optimal exploit policy is non-pure, which requires a tie, e.g.,
w′
xA,uA = w′
xB,uB = maxx,u w′
x,u. This is an equality constraint on (π, γ), e.g., γxA,uAπxA,uA = γxB,uBπxB,uB,
which defines a measure-zero set.
Theorem B.3. The set of world parameters (π, γ) ∈Θ for which the optimal masking policy is also a fair
policy is a set of Lebesgue measure zero.
Proof. The fair policies are a strict subset of the feasible set for the masking problem. Therefore, W(Amask) ⩾
W(Afair). The solutions are identical if and only if the maximum over the larger set happens to lie in the
smaller subset.
15


--- Page 16 ---
The optimal masking policy is a double-threshold strategy: αx,1 = I(wx,1 > λ) and αx,0 = I(wx,0 > −λ),
where wx,u := γx,u Pr(u|x) and λ is a Lagrange multiplier chosen to satisfy the constraint (we ignore measure-
zero cases where the decision is fractional).
For this policy to be fair, it must be that αx,1 = αx,0 for all x. This means that for the optimal λ∗, every
stratum x must have a policy of either (1, 1) or (0, 0). No stratum can have an ”unfair” policy of (1, 0) or
(0, 1). A policy is (1, 0) if wx,1 > λ∗and wx,0 ⩽−λ∗. A policy is (0, 1) if wx,1 ⩽λ∗and wx,0 > −λ∗.
The masking LP is free to select such ”unfair” policies for individual strata if doing so increases the global
objective, provided the choices can be balanced to satisfy the zero ATE constraint. For instance, suppose
stratum xA has a large positive wxA,1 and stratum xB has a large positive wxB,0. The masking LP can
potentially set αxA,1 = 1 and αxB,0 = 1 (and others zero), yielding a high objective value. This policy
is unfair. It is feasible for the masking problem if Pr(X = xA)(1 −0) + Pr(X = xB)(0 −1) = 0, i.e., if
Pr(X = xA) = Pr(X = xB).
The optimal masking solution is fair only if, for the given parameters (π, γ), no such profitable ”unfair”
combination of choices exists. The condition where an unfair policy (like the one above) gives an objective
value *equal to* the optimal fair policy’s objective value is an equality constraint on the parameters (π, γ).
For instance:
γxA,1πxA,1 + γxB,0πxB,0 = max
x
(γx,1πx,1 + γx,0πx,0)
Such equations define the boundaries between regions in the parameter space Θ where different types of
policies are optimal. These boundaries are lower-dimensional manifolds and thus have Lebesgue measure
zero. For a generic choice of parameters, one side of the inequality will hold, and it is overwhelmingly likely
that there exists some profitable, unfair trade-off. The masking solution will thus almost never be a fair
one.
C
The Geometry of Fair and Masked Policies
Let the world of interest be defined by a policy vector ⃗α ∈[0, 1]2k, with constraints carving out a feasible
region within this hypercube. The size of this region represents the “freedom” the optimizer has to find a
good solution. When constraints are perfectly enforced (ε = 0), the feasible sets become lower-dimensional
affine subspaces. Their dimension quantifies their size.
Theorem C.1 (Dimensionality of Feasible Sets). Let Vfair and Vmask be the feasible sets for the perfectly
fair and masking problems (εfair = εmask = 0), respectively.
1. Containment: Vfair ⊆Vmask.
2. Dimensionality: The dimension of Vfair is k −1, while the dimension of Vmask is 2k −2.
3. Growth of Freedom: The masking problem has k−1 more dimensions of freedom than the fair problem.
Proof. (1) Any policy satisfying the k local fairness constraints, αx,1 −αx,0 = 0, also satisfies the global
masking constraint, P
x Pr(X = x)(αx,1−αx,0) = 0. (2) The policy space is 2k-dimensional. The fair problem
imposes k local fairness constraints and 1 global participation constraint, for a total of k + 1 independent
linear equality constraints. The resulting dimension is 2k −(k + 1) = k −1. The masking problem imposes 1
global ATE constraint and 1 global participation constraint, for 2 total constraints. The dimension is 2k −2.
(3) The difference is (2k −2) −(k −1) = k −1.
This result shows that the masking policy’s advantage, in terms of the dimensionality of its search space,
grows linearly with the number of strata, k. When ε > 0, the constraints define “slabs” instead of thin
hyperplanes, and the feasible sets become full-dimensional bodies. We can compare their volumes.
Proposition C.2 (Volume of Feasible Sets). Let Vol(V (ε)) be the volume of the feasible set for a given
ε > 0. For a small ε, the volumes scale as:
1. Vol(Vfair(ε)) ∝εk
2. Vol(Vmask(ε)) ∝ε1
16


--- Page 17 ---
Proof (Sketch). The ε-fair problem constrains the policy vector ⃗α to lie within k independent slabs, each
of width proportional to ε. The volume of their intersection thus scales with εk. The ε-masking problem
imposes only a single slab constraint, so its volume scales linearly with ε.
The ratio of the feasible volumes, Vol(Vmask)/Vol(Vfair) ∝1/εk−1, is therefore immense. This disparity in
“freedom to operate” provides a clear geometric reason for the masking policy’s ability to find significantly
better solutions.
D
Hypothesis Tests
D.1
Testing for Zero ATE
In the setting where P is binary, we employ a Z-test to test the null hypothesis HATE : ATE = 0. The test
statistic is
[
ATE =
k
X
i=1
ˆpxi ˆτxi
where
ˆpx = 1
n
n
X
i=1
1{Xi=x},
x ∈{0, 1},
and
ˆτx =
P
i:Xi=x, Pi=1 Di
P
i:Xi=x, Pi=1 1 −
P
i:Xi=x, Pi=0 Di
P
i:Xi=x, Pi=0 1 .
The Z-statistic is then Z = [
ATE
c
SE([
ATE), which is asymptotically standard normal under the null hypoth-
esis HATE. We use a significance level of 5%.
D.2
Testing for Conditional Independence
A more nuanced way to detect unfairness is to directly test the null hypothesis of conditional independence;
i.e., HCATE : D ⊥P | X. To evaluate this, first for each stratum, x ∈{1, ..., k −1}, we apply Fisher’s exact
test [McDonald, 2014] for independence to obtain a corresponding p-value.
To test the joint null hypothesis that conditional independence holds across all strata, we combine the
per-stratum p-values using Fisher’s method,
S = −2
X
x∈ν
log px,
where the ν denotes the set of strata with observations in the P = 1 and P = 0 groups. Under HCATE, the
combined statistics satisfy S ∼χ2
2|ν|. We reject the null hypothesis when the combined p-value falls below
the 5% significance level.
17
